Summary: In somatic-to-neuron conversion, activation of neuron-specific transcription factors such as NEUROD1 is involved in transforming somatic cells into neurons. A cascade of upstream proteins activates NEUROD1 and leads to transcription of downstream genes. Transcription of the downstream genes culminates in the transformation of somatic cells into neurons.
Reference: Jastrzębski, M. K., Wójcik, P., Stępnicki, P., & Kaczor, A. A. (2024). Effects of small molecules on neurogenesis: Neuronal proliferation and differentiation. Acta Pharm Sin B, 14(1), 20-37. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsb.2023.10.007

Summary: NEUROD1 functions as a transcription factor in Xenopus embryos and mice and mediates neuronal fate specification and neuronal differentiation. Studies in Caenorhabditis elegans indicate that NEUROD1 homologs are critical regulators of neuronal progenitor cell differentiation and neuronal specification. Studies in Drosophila melanogaster indicate that NEUROD1 homologs are critical regulators of neuronal progenitor cell differentiation and neuronal specification. In pluripotent mouse ES cells and in the E13.5 mouse cortex NEUROD1 directly binds promoters and enhancers of neuronal developmental genes. NEUROD1 direct binding to regulatory elements enables it to override the pluripotent state and promote neurogenesis. In mice NEUROD1 is required for normal neurogenesis in vestibular and spiral ganglia and Neurod1 deletion causes substantial defects in neurogenesis of sensory neurons in the vestibular and spiral ganglia. In the inner ear NEUROD1 is essential for initiation of neurogenesis, delamination of neuroblasts, and survival of early neurons. In the olfactory epithelium elimination of NEUROD1 does not affect initial olfactory sensory neuron differentiation but compromises production and survival of mature neurons. In the retina of Neurod1-deficient mice rod and cone photoreceptors progressively degenerate and amacrine cell differentiation is delayed. In Xenopus NEUROD1 was initially characterized for converting nonneural ectodermal cells into neurons.
Reference: Pavlinkova, G., & Smolik, O. (2024). NEUROD1: transcriptional and epigenetic regulator of human and mouse neuronal and endocrine cell lineage programs. Front Cell Dev Biol, 12, 1435546. https://doi.org/10.3389/fcell.2024.1435546

Summary:
Systematic: role(context) → mechanism → outcome.

AAV NeuroD1 infection in injured mouse cortex after stroke induces astrocyte-to-neuron conversion. Converted cells were NeuN-positive and were distributed in superficial (layers II–III) and deep (layers V–VI) cortical layers. Most NeuroD1-converted neurons expressed cortical pyramidal markers Emx1, Tbr1, and Satb2. About 10% of NeuroD1-converted neurons were positive for GABAergic markers parvalbumin and GABA. Total neuronal density in peri-injury areas increased to about 74% of non-stroke levels. Non-converted neurons more than doubled versus control, indicating enhanced survival, and the NeuroD1-treated cortex showed a smaller injury core and a thicker cortex. At 60 dpi, NeuroD1-GFP–labeled converted neurons fired repetitive action potentials and displayed spontaneous excitatory and inhibitory synaptic events, suggesting that these neurons have formed synaptic connections with other neurons and that synaptic responses were rescued versus controls.
Reference: Chen, Y. C., Ma, N. X., Pei, Z. F., Wu, Z., Do-Monte, F. H., Keefe, S., Yellin, E., Chen, M. S., Yin, J. C., Lee, G., Minier-Toribio, A., Hu, Y., Bai, Y. T., Lee, K., Quirk, G. J., & Chen, G. (2020). A NeuroD1 AAV-Based Gene Therapy for Functional Brain Repair after Ischemic Injury through In Vivo Astrocyte-to-Neuron Conversion. Mol Ther, 28(1), 217-234. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymthe.2019.09.003

Summary: NEUROD1 is required for spiral ganglion neuron development in mice. Neurog1 functions upstream of NEUROD1 and Neurog1 and NEUROD1 truncate Atoh1 expression. NEUROD1 negatively regulates Atoh1 in the cerebellum. Loss of NEUROD1 in the vestibular ganglion resulted in Atoh1‑positive hair‑cell‑like cells within intraganglionic vesicles and suggested conversion of vestibular sensory neurons into hair cells. In double‑null mutants lacking both Atoh1 and NEUROD1, the ectopic hair cells are not formed, indicating a NEUROD1–Atoh1 interaction is required for neuronal conversion into ectopic hair cells. In NEUROD1 null mice many sensory neurons were lost but some survived. Surviving neurons showed intermingled vestibular and auditory projections to cochlear hair cells and reduced and aberrant central projections to cochlear nuclei. Spiral ganglion neurons depend upon NEUROD1 and NEUROD1 null mice retain residual spiral ganglion neurons that extend centrally to smaller cochlear nuclei. The length of the cochlea depends on NEUROD1.
Reference: Elliott, K. L., Pavlinkova, G., Chizhikov, V. V., Yamoah, E. N., & Fritzsch, B. (2021). Neurog1, Neurod1, and Atoh1 are essential for spiral ganglia, cochlear nuclei, and cochlear hair cell development. Fac Rev, 10, 47. https://doi.org/10.12703/r/10-47

Summary: NEUROD1 was used to generate neurons from T cells under a straightforward, glial cell-free condition. The protocol overexpressed NEUROD1 together with OCT3/4, SOX2, KLF4, and c-MYC without fully inducing an iPSC state. NEUROD1, a neuronal bHLH factor, was used to help overcome epigenetic barriers in the reprogramming of peripheral blood cells into neurons. Single-cell RNA sequencing supported direct conversion of peripheral blood cells into neurons without induced pluripotent stem cell intermediates. Whole-genome bisulfite sequencing supported direct conversion of peripheral blood cells into neurons without induced pluripotent stem cell intermediates. Lineage-tracing experiments using the Cre-LoxP system supported direct conversion of peripheral blood cells into neurons without induced pluripotent stem cell intermediates. The combination of NEUROD1, OCT3/4, SOX2, KLF4, and c-MYC generated glutamatergic neurons from peripheral blood cells within three weeks and achieved high-efficiency neuronal conversion that yielded nearly as many neurons as the initially seeded T cells. The method introduced genes via viral vectors and improved cell survival.
Reference: Saito, Y., Ishikawa, M., Ohkuma, M., Moody, J., Mabuchi, Y., Sanosaka, T., Ando, Y., Yamashita, T., Hon, C. C., Shin, J. W., Akamatsu, W., & Okano, H. (2025). NEUROD1 efficiently converts peripheral blood cells into neurons with partial reprogramming by pluripotency factors. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A, 122(18), e2401387122. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2401387122
